text: From the perspective of topography and hydrological conditions, the region has a high west and low east terrain, which provides a basis for the eastward infiltration of river water. The Huan River flows through the Linzhou Basin, where the water collection conditions are favorable. As it reaches the Xiejiaping area, the riverbed slope becomes gentler, extending the water flow retention time. Additionally, the riverbed elevation is higher than the water level of the Nanhai Spring, forcing groundwater to flow eastward and discharge in the river valley near the Nanhai Spring group. The amount of leakage is related to the river flow. Aside from atmospheric precipitation, the discharge from upstream factories and mines, as well as the abandoned water from the Hongqi Canal, leads to variable flow rates. Measurements show that the average leakage rate from Woshanshan to Hengshui is approximately 0.2 m³/(s・km), while from Hengshui to Xiejiaping it is about 0.14 m³/(s・km).

From the perspective of geological and structural conditions, the Shuimushan - Hengshui - Xiejiaping section is the main leakage segment. The banks of this section are predominantly composed of Quaternary loess-like sub-sandy soil and other deposits, underlain by Ordovician limestone. In some areas, the bedrock is exposed, and the limestone is fractured with numerous fissures and solution cavities, providing pathways for river water leakage. The Ordovician limestone in this area is widespread and highly soluble, with the river water being of the HCO₃ - Ca - Mg type and possessing erosive capabilities, which meet the conditions for karst development. The development of karst has become the dominant factor in river water leakage. Additionally, the region features numerous high-angle normal faults oriented north-northeast, with the Xinhua-Xia fault exhibiting compressive torsion and local bundling, which leads to the fracturing of rock layers, the development of karst, and a tendency towards drainage areas. Furthermore, the presence of many monoclinal rock layers in the area facilitates groundwater infiltration, further promoting river water leakage.

The study of the leakage patterns of the Dagu River channel was conducted through the determination of unsaturated seepage characteristics of sand samples, indoor infiltration tests, and field infiltration tests. In the indoor vertical infiltration tests, the initial moisture content and texture of the sand samples affected infiltration. The vertical infiltration rate decreased with increasing initial moisture content, increased with larger sand particle sizes, and decreased with higher dry bulk density. The infiltration rate varied in stages over time, with the stable infiltration rate of coarse sand being greater than that of fine sand. For instance, when the initial moisture content of coarse sand was 9.30% and 0.44%, the vertical stable infiltration rates were approximately 0.002 cm/s and 0.0022 cm/s, respectively. For fine sand, with initial moisture contents of 9.65% and 2.50%, the vertical stable infiltration rates were about 0.0018 cm/s and 0.002 cm/s. The indoor lateral seepage tests indicated that the initial moisture content and texture of the sand samples influenced lateral infiltration. The lateral infiltration rate increased with higher initial moisture content and larger sand particle sizes, while it decreased with higher dry bulk density. The infiltration rate initially increased, then decreased until it stabilized, with different lateral stable infiltration rates for coarse and fine sand samples at varying initial moisture contents. Field infiltration tests revealed that the hydraulic properties of the unsaturated zone sand samples affected the infiltration capacity of the riverbed. Seasonal river channel infiltration was divided into unstable and stable phases, with coarser sand samples exhibiting shorter unstable infiltration phases. The stable infiltration rates varied across different test sites, and the cumulative infiltration amount changed regularly over time, with the proportion of unstable infiltration decreasing over time.

The Dagu River features a wide and shallow sandy riverbed, with an irregular cross-section and strong permeability, often remaining in a state of desiccation. The leakage recharge during flood periods directly affects both the flood flow and the amount of groundwater recharge. According to data from 1981 to 1989, except for 1985, the river experienced interruptions during most periods, with all the water between Nancun and Nanzhangyuan infiltrating to recharge the groundwater. During the water release experiments from 1981 to 1989, the majority of the total water released was infiltrated water, which ultimately recharged the groundwater. Long-term hydrological observation data indicates that the Dagu River is a typical seasonal river, characterized by high peak floods and rapid rises and falls. During the flow or flood periods, the leakage from the river channel is significant, especially during the flood season, where most of the water loss along the route is attributed to river channel leakage.

The results indicate that riverbed leakage affects the flood evolution process, manifested as reduced flow, lowered water levels, and delayed peak occurrence times at the same cross-section. The primary factor influencing this is the infiltration rate of the riverbed; the greater the infiltration rate, the more pronounced the effect. Changes in the riverbed slope have little impact on the calculation results. For example, when calculating the flow in a prismatic river channel, variations in the infiltration rate lead to corresponding changes in flow and water level processes at each cross-section, as well as along the flow path.

By analyzing the recharge and discharge relationships of the Jin Ci spring area, the recharge zone is located in the extensive carbonate exposed area north of the ancient Jiao Fen River, covering an area of 391 km². The Fen River crosses the middle and northern parts of the Jin Ci spring area, from Luojiaqu to the boundary of the spring area, with the riverbed base consisting of carbonate rock at different locations, where surface water seeps to recharge groundwater. South of the Fen River, extending to Jiaocheng and Qingxu, the upper surface of the Ordovician limestone is covered by Carboniferous-Permian clastic rock, with thickness gradually increasing from north to south. Due to the influence of the Malan syncline and the Shiganfeng syncline within the area, the southwestern part of the spring area is a deep burial zone of Ordovician limestone, where karst development is poor, forming a groundwater stagnation area. In contrast, in the eastern and southeastern parts of the spring area, particularly around Wangfeng, Baijiazhuang, Kaihua, and Jin Ci, located on the eastern wing of the Shiganfeng syncline, the depth of the Ordovician limestone becomes shallower. Additionally, influenced by the NNE-trending fault zones, karst development of the Ordovician limestone is prominent, forming a strong groundwater runoff zone in the eastern part of the spring area. The Jin Ci spring is situated within this strong runoff zone, and from a tectonic geological perspective, the Jin Ci to Yayu fault zone trends NNE and consists of three faults that form a stepped fault structure from west to east. Exposed on both sides of the fault are Ordovician limestone, with only a Carboniferous-Permian cover layer at the top of the hill, indicating that the Ordovician karst aquifer is relatively shallow in the Jin Ci area. On the eastern side of the fault, there are Quaternary loose layers, where karst groundwater encounters clay rock barriers to the east, causing it to rise along the fault and surface as spring water, typical of a mountain front fault overflow spring. Within the Jin Ci spring area, the two strong leakage sections upstream of the Fen River are from Luojiaqu to the Zhenchengdi section, approximately 20 km long, and from Zhaizhang to Saoshi, approximately 24 km long, with a total length of about 44 km. The seepage from the river channel directly contributes to the karst groundwater of the Jin Ci spring area.

River channel leakage is an important aspect of water environment protection and the conversion of water resources. The study of surface water replenishing groundwater through river channel leakage holds significant practical importance for the groundwater replenishment in inland basins. The main research methods for river channel leakage, both domestically and internationally, include field testing, empirical formulas, analytical methods, and numerical methods. The field testing method is straightforward and direct, serving as the most fundamental approach to measuring and calculating river channel leakage; however, it is limited by certain experimental conditions. Various countries have proposed different empirical formulas to calculate river channel leakage losses during their water conservancy development, which are typically characterized by their simplicity and ease of application. However, in practical applications, the arbitrary selection of coefficients in these formulas and the lack of consideration for temporal and spatial variations in leakage limit their applicability and promotion. Academician Xue Yuqun from the Chinese Academy of Sciences derived a calculation formula for stable river channel leakage using analytical methods, but it is only applicable under specific conditions and involves complex parameters and calculations. The numerical simulation method is the primary approach for studying groundwater issues through model simulation. Due to the complexity of groundwater models, numerical methods are often required. Despite decades of research, some problems remain unresolved. Thus, it is evident that the research on river channel leakage calculation methods, both domestically and internationally, shows that different methods have their own applicable conditions and scopes.

For the Jin Ci Spring area, the most suitable method to determine the leakage amount of the river channel is based on the principle of water balance. This involves calculating the leakage amount for a segment by taking the difference in flow rates upstream and downstream of the leakage section, subtracting the inflow to the segment and the amount of water extracted by humans. However, the existing data comes from three hydrological stations: Fenhe Reservoir Station, Zhaishang Station, and Lanchun Station, which provide measured flow data. Given the hydrological conditions of the Jin Ci Spring area, several larger first-order tributaries of the Fenhe River are seasonal rivers, making it impossible to ascertain the magnitude of inflow during the flood season and the amount of human extraction during that period. Consequently, it is not feasible to calculate the leakage amount using the existing data based on the water balance principle. Therefore, we reference previous research on the relationship between the leakage amount of the Fenhe River in the Jin Ci Spring area and the upstream inflow amount, which establishes a relationship between upstream inflow and river channel leakage without considering the inflow to the segment. This allows us to calculate the percentage of river channel leakage relative to upstream inflow, known as the leakage coefficient, which serves as a basis for analyzing the relationship between the leakage coefficient and upstream inflow when calculating the leakage amount for the Fenhe River's leakage section. River channel leakage is influenced by multiple factors; aside from geological lithology, structure, and hydrogeological conditions, the primary influence comes from the magnitude of inflow to the river channel. There is a good correspondence between the runoff of the Fenhe River and the leakage amount of the river channel. The amount of inflow to the river channel determines the extent of leakage; as inflow increases, the leakage coefficient decreases non-linearly, while it increases non-linearly as flow decreases, with a significant increase at low flow rates and a smaller increase at high flow rates.

The monitoring section of the Wind River uses the dynamic water method to determine river leakage due to upstream gate leakage and downstream outflow. The basic principle is that the change in water volume in the river over a certain period is equal to the difference between the changes in water volume at the upstream and downstream sections and the amounts of infiltration and evaporation. River leakage is expressed as follows: Q = Qλ - Q_out - E + ΔQ. In this equation, Q represents the infiltration amount (m³/d), Qλ is the inflow at the upstream section (m³/d), Q_out is the outflow at the downstream section (m³/d), E is the evaporation amount from the water surface (m³/d), and ΔQ is the change in water volume caused by changes in river water level. The monitoring section of the Cha River and the Beiyecang main canal does not have gate leakage, so the static water method is used for measurement, with the leakage formula as: Q = ΔQ - E. This method indirectly estimates the leakage amount by observing changes in water level and is suitable for river sections without significant inflow and outflow conditions.

After the channel leakage reaches a stable phase, the leakage amount varies with the water level. The curve of channel water level Z changing with time t, denoted as Z=f(t), is called the leakage characteristic curve. Its first derivative f′(t)=dZ/dt represents the rate of water level decline, reflecting the magnitude of the leakage amount. When the groundwater depth is relatively large, the seepage gradient can be considered constant, and the leakage flow rate has a linear relationship with water depth, resulting in a straight line for the leakage characteristic curve. When the groundwater level is higher, the buoyancy effect is significant, and the leakage flow rate varies non-linearly with water depth. In the Nanhongmen irrigation area, where the groundwater depth is large and the buoyancy effect is weak, the leakage characteristic curves for the Chahhe and Beiyechang main channels are both straight lines, with good fitting results (R²>0.8).

Infiltration intensity refers to the amount of seepage water per unit time, per unit length of channel section, and per unit wetted perimeter. During the initial phase of water flow in the channel, the infiltration intensity is relatively high, gradually decreasing over time and stabilizing. Under a constant water level, the variation of infiltration intensity over time can be expressed by the formula q=ab·1/t^k; under varying water levels, the relationship between infiltration intensity and water depth is given by q=aH^b. The infiltration measurement for the bifurcation river and the Beiyechang main channel was conducted using the variable water level hydrostatic method, resulting in fitted equations for the relationship between infiltration intensity and water depth: for the bifurcation river, q=2×10^−6·h^13.30, and for the Beiyechang main channel, q=0.163·h^8.311, which can be used to calculate infiltration amounts at any water level.

Infiltration rate refers to the amount of water that infiltrates into the soil per unit area over a unit time, and its value is equal to the flux of surface soil moisture movement. Under the same soil conditions, the infiltration rate increases with increasing water depth. When the channel water is disconnected from groundwater, the recharge is primarily through vertical infiltration, and the Darcy's law for unsaturated soil is: q = −K(θ)·dψ/dz. An increase in water depth leads to an increase in pressure potential, which in turn increases the absolute value of the soil water potential gradient, resulting in a higher infiltration rate. The infiltration rates of the bifurcation river and the Beiya field dry channel exhibit exponential growth with water depth, with the fitting equations being i = 7×10^−11·e^(4.715h) and i = 1×10^−10·e^(6.255h).

Based on the parameters of the monitored section, the total infiltration volume of the entire river is estimated by calculating the product of infiltration intensity, wetted perimeter, time, and channel length. The total annual infiltration volume of the Cha River is 1,042,400 m³, while the annual infiltration volume of the Beiyechang main canal, under normal water depth conditions (20 cm), is only 81.6 L, which can be considered negligible. Due to the poor accuracy of the dynamic water method and the small range of water level fluctuations, an SPSS software T-test analysis indicates that the annual infiltration volume is 2,960,500 m³. The total annual leakage volume of the irrigation district's river channel is 4,002,900 m³, with an error of only 3.6% compared to the numerical simulation result (4,153,500 m³), indicating that the measured data is reliable.

The Dagu River is one of the main water sources for Qingdao City and is an important source of water for domestic use as well as industrial and agricultural purposes. Flooding in the Dagu River mostly occurs in July and August, typically lasting 3 to 5 days. The flooding in the main stream directly affects the infiltration recharge of groundwater, influencing both the flood flow and the amount of groundwater recharge. Studying the recharge and discharge patterns of groundwater in the Dagu River channel provides a scientific basis for establishing the temporal and spatial relationship between flow (especially peak flood flow) and groundwater infiltration, which is essential for the development of a joint scheduling system for flood control in the Dagu River basin. Additionally, it offers insights for research on river basin scheduling in other seasons. This study conducted investigations along the Dagu River in meteorology, hydrology, and geology. Based on an understanding of domestic and international research findings on channel infiltration, a combined method of experimental analysis and numerical simulation was employed to explore the infiltration patterns of the river channel and their influencing factors under different conditions.

Representative sections were selected for unsaturated infiltration tests in the upper and middle reaches of the river channel. Through field in-situ experiments, the infiltration flux patterns at different locations in the middle reaches of the river were determined. Three in-situ tests measured the stable flux at Yujiaxiaoli to be 0.028 ml/(s*cm²); at Wangbi, it was 0.010 ml/(s*cm²); and at Nanshaliang Village, it was 0.023 ml/(s*cm²). The infiltration flux of sandy soil in the upper reaches of the river is greater than that in the middle reaches; within the upper reaches, the closer the distance to the river channel, the better the permeability of the vadose zone, resulting in a larger infiltration flux.

The Dagu River features a wide and shallow sandy riverbed with an irregular cross-section and strong permeability. The riverbed often remains dry, with significant flooding occurring only during the rainy season; at other times, the main river channel is essentially dry. The leakage and recharge of the Dagu River directly influence the flood discharge and the volume of groundwater replenishment. Building on previous research on flood control and hydrology in the Dagu River, this paper establishes the temporal and spatial relationship between the river flow (particularly peak flood flow) and groundwater leakage through field monitoring and various indoor and outdoor simulation experiments, providing a scientific basis for the establishment of a joint scheduling system for flood storage and river management in the Dagu River basin.

The Dagu River runs from north to south through the entire area, with a close relationship between river water and groundwater. The "windows" along the river allow for communication between the upper and lower sand layers, connecting river water and groundwater to form a unified water body, creating very favorable conditions for the mutual transformation of the "two waters." The transformation relationship between river water and groundwater depends on the relative levels of both. When the groundwater level is higher than the river water level, groundwater discharges into the river, transforming into surface water; conversely, when the groundwater level is lower than the river water level, the river water first connects with the groundwater level through vertical free infiltration, and then laterally permeates into the aquifers on both sides of the river, gradually transforming into groundwater. Thus, there exists a relationship of mutual replenishment and discharge, that is, mutual transformation between river water and groundwater in this area.

River channel leakage is a complex hydrodynamic process that can be broadly divided into four stages: a. In the initial stage of flow, vertical free infiltration occurs; in wide and shallow channels, lateral leakage can be ignored due to the width being much greater than the depth; b. As time progresses, the vadose zone reaches field capacity, the groundwater level beneath the river rises, and the leakage rate decreases, reaching what is hydrologically referred to as the stable infiltration rate, at which point lateral infiltration can still be ignored; c. When the flow duration continues to extend, the vadose zone becomes saturated, meaning the groundwater level rises to the riverbed, transitioning to buoyant leakage, where the infiltration rate becomes the permeability coefficient. Since the hydraulic gradient is still changing, it remains non-stable infiltration; at this stage, the lateral seepage increases, but vertical seepage remains predominant; d. The final stage occurs when the river water level continues to rise, with lateral seepage becoming dominant, and the seepage rate primarily related to the lateral hydraulic gradient.

In the Dagu River basin, due to large-scale sand extraction and soil erosion, many river sections, especially those downstream, have lost much of their unsaturated zone of sandy soil, or only have a very thin layer of soil remaining. The river water replenishes the groundwater mainly through lateral infiltration on both sides. To study the lateral unsaturated infiltration patterns over a larger area, a lateral soil tank model experiment was designed.

In-situ tests for river infiltration were conducted by taking measurements along the banks of the Dagu River. The changes in lateral infiltration flux over time were measured at different riverbed sections. Comparisons indicate that the sand infiltration flux in the upstream section of the Dagu River is greater than that in the middle section; the closer the test site is to the river, the better the permeability of the unsaturated zone, resulting in a higher infiltration flux. The results from three in-situ tests are as follows: the maximum flux at Yujia Xiaoli was 0.090 ml/(s*cm²) with a stable flux of 0.028 ml/(s*cm²); at Wangbi, the maximum flux was 0.035 ml/(s*cm²) with a stable flux of 0.010 ml/(s*cm²); and at Nanshaliang Village, the maximum flux was 0.075 ml/(s*cm²) with a stable flux of 0.023 ml/(s*cm²).
